content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Adapted into English from my original Japanese article, with AI assistance for translation and editing. The worry is fully mine, and it hasn’t gone away.
In my last piece, I wrote about our AI adoption in glowing terms. Revenue up, work cycles multiplied, long-abandoned tasks recovered. All of it true.
But a few months into this transformation, there is exactly one worry I can’t resolve. Today is that story. I’ll apologize in advance: no solution appears at the end.
What intuition actually is
In Japanese workplaces we have a word: kan (勘). It’s the thing a veteran feels half a second after seeing a spreadsheet — this number is wrong — before any reasoning arrives. English calls it gut feeling, intuition, a nose for the business. Sixteen years in e-commerce gave me mine.
I don’t consider it mystical. I think kan is integrated analysis running unconsciously — many signals cross-referenced against years of accumulated experience, below the surface of awareness. The reasoning is real; it just doesn’t announce itself. When I once audited my own cognition with personality assessments, the conclusion pointed the same way: cross-domain integration is where I actually work.
The part that matters for this article: that unconscious integration engine is built one way only. Repetition. Years of hands-on, unglamorous, repetitive work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What AI analyzes well — and what it still can’t
Let me be precise, because I’m not here to underrate the machines. AI’s single-thread analysis is already superb. Why did this product’s conversion rate drop? Where is this ad campaign wasting spend? On questions like these it’s faster than me and more accurate.
What it still fumbles is integration across domains. The newest models are better. They are not there.
A concrete example from my floor. On marketplaces, search ranking is driven overwhelmingly by sales history. So if you hunt for the next winning product using historical data alone, you fail — a product with no track record isn’t necessarily a loser; it may simply not have soil to grow in yet. Consider what the judgment actually requires:

• Low-traffic products have conversion rates too noisy for statistics to grip

• Baseline conversion differs by category, so no single threshold means anything

• And the decisive call: “if we buy this product one burst of sales with ads, it will carry itself afterward”
That last call is still human work. It means standing in a statistics-free zone and integrating the product itself, the mood of the market, and the habits of the category into a verdict: this will sell. Hand the decision to AI and you get an endless parade of safe, proven, mediocre picks.
The human role is migrating from “correcting” to “integrating”
I still correct the AI’s analysis sometimes. The classic case: outliers left inside period averages — a flash-sale spike or a stockout window quietly counted as normal days.
Here’s the interesting part: the corrections keep getting rarer. Every fix becomes a rule it retains. The AI is genuinely growing. And so the human contribution keeps sliding along one axis — away from error-hunting, toward the integrated judgment AI can’t yet reach.
Up to this point, it’s a good-news story. Now the problem.
Where does the next generation grow their kan?
My kan was built by years of grunt work. Compiling numbers by hand. Getting fooled by outliers. Watching products I knew would sell go nowhere, and products I dismissed take off. That humiliating repetition is the raw material of the integration engine.
That repetition is now the AI’s job.
The compiling, the reports, the routine analyses that used to belong to juniors and mid-level staff — at our company, nearly all of it is automated. Department productivity is visibly up. But say it plainly: the practice hours where intuition gets built have been deleted from the schedule.
Ten years from now, the integrated judgment calls will presumably still need humans — the AI frontier moves, but so does the difficulty of what’s left. Who will be standing there, able to make them? Where, exactly, were they supposed to have practiced?
I don’t have a countermeasure
Honestly: I have no answer to this question.
We’re only months into serious AI adoption. Maybe this is a needless panic. Maybe working alongside AI is itself a new form of hands-on experience, and it will grow a kind of professional I can’t yet imagine. “I don’t know” is the accurate statement.
But if I’m going to brag about the efficiency numbers — and I have — then intellectual honesty requires posting this worry in the same ledger. As a manager, I am currently choosing today’s productivity over a training pipeline for the people of 2036, and I do not yet know how to stop doing that.
Three questions to run on your own job — 1. Your professional intuition — which streams of information is it integrating? 2. Which repetitive, hands-on work built it? 3. Will that work still be done by human hands in five years?
If your answer to the third is “probably not,” this isn’t an e-commerce story. It’s your industry’s story, on a delay.
How is your workplace handling this? Are you protecting training time somehow, or carrying the same unresolved worry I am? I read the responses, and I’m planning a follow-up built on them.
This is part of an ongoing series on AI in real e-commerce operations, originally documented in Japanese. Follow along for the next installment.
